## Overview
Sehoon Ha is a researcher at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he conducts interdisciplinary work at the intersection of **robotics**, **computer graphics**, and **machine learning**. He contributes to and leads projects spanning algorithmic foundations, system-level toolkits, and real-world robot deployment. His work emphasizes <u>deep reinforcement learning</u>, <u>legged locomotion and terrain-aware navigation</u>, and <u>sim-to-real transfer and robot design co-optimization</u>. Across these themes he develops both principled learning algorithms and practical engineering artifacts—including *open-source toolkits* and *deployable locomotion systems*—that bridge simulation and hardware.

---

## Research Areas
Sehoon Ha’s research centers on developing scalable learning methods for embodied agents and robots. He has produced influential work on model-free and safe deep reinforcement learning (for example, the highly cited "Soft actor-critic algorithms and applications" and follow-up safety-oriented papers such as "Learning to be safe: Deep rl with a safety critic"), and on applying RL to complex motor skills for legged platforms ("Learning to walk via deep reinforcement learning", "Learning to walk in the real world with minimal human effort", "Legged robots that keep on learning"). He also advances tools and methods for bridging simulation and real robots—creating and maintaining *differentiable and modular simulation frameworks* ("Dart: Dynamic animation and robotics toolkit", "Pods: Policy optimization via differentiable simulation") and techniques for sim-to-real robustness (residual physics, system identification, and policy fine-tuning). More recently his work extends into semantic and language-informed navigation and long-horizon task composition ("Vlfm: Vision-language frontier maps for zero-shot semantic navigation", "Asc: Adaptive skill coordination for robotic mobile manipulation"), as well as meta-learning and design co-optimization for jointly improving robot morphology and controllers ("Joint Optimization of Robot Design and Motion Parameters", "Computational co-optimization of design parameters and motion trajectories"). Across these areas he combines algorithmic innovation, learning-based control, perception for locomotion, and engineering frameworks that enable reproducible transfer to hardware.

---

## Notable Works
- "Soft actor-critic algorithms and applications" (2018, 4462 citations) — seminal contributions to practical, sample-efficient model-free RL that have become foundational for continuous-control problems and subsequently informed numerous robotics and control applications.
- "Learning to walk via deep reinforcement learning" (2018, 683 citations) and follow-on deployment papers — a program of research demonstrating end-to-end learned locomotion policies, sim-to-real transfer, and minimal-intervention real-world training for legged robots.
- "Dart: Dynamic animation and robotics toolkit" (2018, 385 citations) — an open-source, cross-disciplinary simulation and dynamics library that has supported research in both computer graphics and robotics, enabling reproducible experiments and integration with learning pipelines.

---

## Academic Background
Sehoon Ha is based at Georgia Institute of Technology and is affiliated with research groups that connect the Graphics Lab and Humanoid Robotics Lab (as evidenced by collaborative tool development and multi-lab authorship). His publication record spans top-tier conferences and journals in robotics, graphics, and machine learning, and his work has been widely adopted and cited (total citations: 9,315). He has collaborated with researchers across institutions (including industry and university partners) and contributed widely used codebases and datasets, reflecting a sustained emphasis on reproducibility and impact. For a complete list of publications, code, and project pages, see his homepage: http://cc.gatech.edu/~sha9.

Q_4d9N0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Greg Turk is a **Professor** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group that spans *computer graphics, scientific visualization, and robotics*. His work centers on the interplay between geometric modeling, physical simulation, and data-driven methods, with particular emphasis on <u>surface reconstruction and representation</u>, <u>texture and pattern synthesis</u>, and <u>physically based animation</u>. He maintains an active laboratory web presence (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~turk/) and has a long-standing record of highly cited contributions (Total citations: **24,547**) that bridge foundational geometry processing techniques and modern learning- and simulation-driven approaches.

---

## Research Areas
Turk's research covers a broad set of topics in **computer graphics** and adjacent fields, with recurring themes that appear across his most-cited papers. In geometric processing and modeling he has advanced methods for <u>surface reconstruction</u> and remeshing (e.g., "Zippered polygon meshes from range images" (1994); "Re-tiling polygonal surfaces" (1992)), and introduced robust implicit representations for handling very large point sets ("Multi-level partition of unity implicits" (2005)) as well as variational implicit techniques ("Variational implicit surfaces" (1999)). In texture and appearance modeling he helped establish exemplar-based and patch-based synthesis methods ("Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts" (2003); "Texture synthesis on surfaces" (2001)), and explored geometry-aware texture generation using biologically inspired reaction–diffusion models ("Generating textures on arbitrary surfaces using reaction-diffusion" (1991)). Turk's work in simulation and animation spans fluid/solid coupling, deformable materials, and fracture ("Rigid fluid" (2004); "Animation of fracture by physical modeling" (1991); "Fast viscoelastic behavior with thin features" (2008)), often combining mesh- and volumetric-based approaches ("Simplification and repair of polygonal models using volumetric techniques" (2003)). More recently his publications indicate an engagement with robotic control and sim-to-real learning—bringing together simulation, system identification, and policy transfer ("Preparing for the unknown" (2017); "Sim-to-real transfer for biped locomotion" (2019))—as well as visualization topics such as streamline placement and tensor/vector field design for scientific graphics.

## Notable Works
- Zippered polygon meshes from range images (1994) — introduced robust algorithms for combining multiple range scans into coherent polygonal models, foundational for 3D scanning and reconstruction workflows.  
- Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts (2003) — developed patch-based synthesis with optimal seam stitching using graph cuts, significantly advancing example-based texture and video synthesis.  
- Multi-level partition of unity implicits (2005) — proposed a scalable implicit surface representation for constructing smooth surfaces from very large, noisy point sets, influencing subsequent work in surface reconstruction and compact implicit modeling.

## Academic Background
Greg Turk has been an active researcher and faculty member for multiple decades, publishing influential work from the late 1980s through the present and supervising numerous students and collaborators at Georgia Tech. His publication record spans premier venues in graphics, visualization, and robotics, and his contributions have been widely cited across these communities (Total citations: **24,547**). He has held leadership and service roles common to senior academics—serving on program committees, editorial boards, and conference organizing efforts within ACM SIGGRAPH, IEEE visualization/graphics communities, and related venues—and his career demonstrates sustained interdisciplinary impact across **geometry processing**, *physically based simulation*, and <u>robotic learning and control</u>.
